paper_title: OptiMer: Optimal Distribution Vector Merging Is Better than Data Mixing for Continual Pre-Training

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Continual pre-training is widely used to adapt LLMs to target languages and domains, yet the mixture ratio of training data remains a sensitive hyperparameter that is expensive to tune: they must be fixed before training begins, and a suboptimal choice can waste weeks of compute. In this work, we propose OptiMer, which decouples ratio selection from training: we train one CPT model per dataset, extract each model's distribution vector, which represents the parameter shift induced by that dataset, and search for optimal composition weights post-hoc via Bayesian optimization. Experiments on Gemma 3 27B across languages (Japanese, Chinese) and domains (Math, Code) show that OptiMer consistently outperforms data mixture and model averaging baselines with 15-35 times lower search cost. Key findings reveal that 1) the optimized weights can be interpreted as data mixture ratios, and retraining with these ratios improves data mixture CPT, and 2) the same vector pool can be re-optimized for a given objective without any retraining, producing target-tailored models on demand. Our work establishes that data mixture ratio selection, traditionally a pre-training decision, can be reformulated as a post-hoc optimization over distribution vectors, offering a more flexible paradigm for continual pre-training.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: OPTIMER: Optimal Distribution Vector Merging Is Better than Data Mixing for Continual Pre-Training Haiyue Song and Masao Utiyama National Institute of Information and Communications Technology, Kyoto, Japan {haiyue.song,mutiyama}@nict.go.jp 6 2 0 2 0 3 ] . [ 1 8 5 8 8 2 . 3 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Continual pre-training is widely used to adapt LLMs to target languages and domains, yet the mixture ratio of training data remains sensitive hyperparameter that is expensive to tune: they must be fixed before training begins, and suboptimal choice can waste weeks of compute. In this work, we propose OPTIMER, which decouples ratio selection from training: we train one CPT model per dataset, extract each models distribution vector, which represents the parameter shift induced by that dataset, and search for optimal composition weights posthoc via Bayesian optimization. Experiments on Gemma 3 27B across languages (Japanese, Chinese) and domains (Math, Code) show that OPTIMER consistently outperforms data mixture and model averaging baselines with 1535 lower search cost. Key findings reveal that 1) the optimized weights can be interpreted as data mixture ratios, and retraining with these ratios improves data mixture CPT, and 2) the same vector pool can be re-optimized for given objective without any retraining, producing targettailored models on demand. Our work establishes that data mixture ratio selection, traditionally pre-training decision, can be reformulated as post-hoc optimization over distribution vectors, offering more flexible paradigm for continual pre-training.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Adapting large language models (LLMs) to specific languages and domains is central challenge, driven by demand for both multilingual coverage and domain expertise (Ng et al., 2025; Alnumay et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2026). Continual pre-training (CPT) is common approach for such adaptation (Gururangan et al., 2020; Ibrahim et al., 2024; Yıldız et al., 2025), where the training corpus is typically mixture 1Our code and model will be available at https:// github.com/shyyhs/optimer. Figure 1: Data Mix vs. OPTIMER. (a) Continual pretraining on fixed data mixture requires the mixing ratios {wi} to be specified before training begins. Each attempt costs days to weeks of GPU time. (b) Our approach trains one CPT model per dataset independently and extracts distribution vector τi from each, which are then composed via merge function Φ (e.g. DARELinear) with weights {αi} optimized post-hoc. Each trial completes in minutes. The instruction-tuned vector is additionally merged in both settings. of multiple datasets (Fujii et al., 2024; Dou et al., 2025). However, the mixing ratio of these datasets is critical yet sensitive hyperparameter: suboptimal ratio can degrade model performance (Xie et al., 2023; Ye et al., 2025). Although recent methods estimate ratios via proxy models or small-scale experiments (Xie et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2025; Ye et al., 2025; Cao et al., 2026a), these estimates must be fixed before training begins and cannot be corrected afterward, meaning poor choice may waste days or even weeks of GPU cluster time before its effect becomes apparent. To address this, we propose OPTIMER, which decouples data ratio selection from model training. As illustrated in Figure 1, instead of fixing the data mixture ratio before training, we train separate CPT model on each dataset independently and extract the corresponding distribution vector (the parameter shift from the base PT model) after training. Furthermore, rather than weight averaging, which leads to suboptimal performance (Yadav et al., 2023), OPTIMER searches for optimal merge weights via Bayesian optimization using the Treestructured Parzen Estimator (TPE) (Akiba et al., 2019; Watanabe, 2023). We find vector merging viable because vectors from distinct datasets are approximately orthogonal, allowing linear combination with minimal interference. Experiments on Gemma 3 27B (Team et al., 2025) with distribution vectors across languages (Japanese, Chinese) and domains (Math, Code) show that OPTIMER consistently outperforms data mixture baselines across all dataset combinations, while requiring 1535 lower search time. Moreover, the same collection of distribution vectors can be re-optimized toward different objectives, yielding multiple target-tailored models without any retraining. Our contributions are as follows: We introduce the concept of distribution vectors for CPT and propose OPTIMER, posthoc framework that decouples data ratio selection from model training by optimizing merge weights via Bayesian optimization. Experimental results on 16 benchmarks covering five task groups (English, Japanese, Chinese, Math, Code) show that OPTIMER outperforms data mixture CPT and four model merging methods across three dataset combinations with 1535 lower search cost. It further enables objective-specific re-optimization from single vector pool without any re-CPT. Our analysis reveals that distribution vectors are approximately orthogonal (cosine 0.03 0.31), enabling composition without severe interference. Training dynamics show that CPT trajectories are approximately linear in parameter space, linking merge weights to effective training duration. OPTIMER search dynamics illustrate the sharp nature of the optimization landscape, thus highlighting the necessity of efficient searching rather than grid search. Additionally, optimized weights can serve as interpretable data mixture ratios and can be negative to remove cross-distribution interference.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Continual Pre-training. Adapting pretrained LLM to new languages or domains via CPT is well-studied area (Gururangan et al., 2020; Li and Lee, 2024). It has been applied to language adaptation (Fujii et al., 2024; Dou et al., 2024, 2025) and domain adaptation (Azerbayev et al., 2024; Lozhkov et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2024). Data mixture ratio is an important hyperparameter that largely affects model performance (Li and Lee, 2024; Shi et al., 2024), which motivates work on data mixture optimization. Data Mixture Optimization. Recently, several methods have been proposed to optimize data mixture ratios. DoReMi (Xie et al., 2023) uses distributionally robust optimization on small proxy model to produce domain weights for larger target model. RegMix (Liu et al., 2025) trains many small models on diverse mixtures and fits regression to predict optimal ratios. Ye et al. (2025); Cao et al. (2026a) propose predictive framework that transfers optimal ratios across scales. Despite these advances, such methods must fix the ratio before training. Instead, we propose to adjust ratios post-hoc which avoids retraining. Task Vectors and Model Merging. Ilharco et al. (2023) show that task vectors τ = θftθbase, the difference between fine-tuned model θft and its base model θbase, can be composed via linear arithmetic to add or remove task capabilities, with subsequent work improving merging quality through sign conflict resolution (Yadav et al., 2023) and delta sparsification (Yu et al., 2024). Chat Vector (Huang et al., 2024) applies weight arithmetic to transfer instruction-following capability to CPT-adapted model without additional fine-tuning. Task-specific CPT checkpoint and LoRA adapter merging has also proven effective for finance domain (Ueda et al., 2025) and machine translation (Cao et al., 2026b). These works focus on task-specific transfer rather than improving general capability across multiple distributions. In contrast, our work extends distribution vector composition to the multidistribution CPT setting and achieves general performance improvement. Automatic Merge Weight Search. Several methinods automate the search for merge ratios, cluding test-time entropy minimization over perlayer weights (Yang et al., 2024), evolutionary search (Akiba et al., 2025), and minimizing output divergence between merged and fine-tuned models (Touayouch et al., 2026). They have been applied to at most two to three models or small-scale models due to the computational cost of the highdimensional search spaces or population-based iterations. Most relevant to our work, DEM (Ram et al., 2024) applies grid search over merge weights for SFT task vectors, but the cost of grid search increases exponentially with the number of vectors. Our proposed OPTIMER replaces grid search with Bayesian optimization via TPE, achieving substantially higher theoretical search efficiency.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: We define the notation and introduce distribution vectors in Section 3.1. In Section 3.2, we present OPTIMER, an automatic merge weight optimization approach via Bayesian optimization.

sections.title:
3.1 Preliminaries
Notation. Let θpt ∈ Rd denote the parameters of
a pretrained base model and θit its instruction-tuned
version. Given n data distributions {Di}n
i=1, each
represented by a dataset, continual pre-training on
Di from θpt yields a CPT model θCPTi.
sections.content: Ilharco et al. (2023) define task Task Vectors. vector τ = θft θbase to capture the parameter change induced by fine-tuning, and construct merged model as θmerge = θbase + λτ , where λ is scalar weight. This has been shown effective for adding or removing capabilities in the fine-tuning setting. Distribution Vectors. We extend task vectors to the CPT setting. We define the distribution vector for Di as: τi = θCPTi θpt, (1) which encodes the parameter change induced by distribution Di. Similarly, we extract an IT vector τit = θit θpt from the instruction-tuned model. Since our CPT models are trained from θpt, they lack instruction-following capability, and adding τit recovers this capability without additional supervised fine-tuning (Huang et al., 2024). Multi-Vector Composition. merged model incorporating distributions and instructionfollowing capability is constructed as: θmerge = θpt + αit τit + (cid:88) i=1 αi τi, (2) where {αi}n i=1 and αit are scalar merge weights. Uniform weighting (αi = 1/n) is natural baseline but leads to suboptimal performance in practice, as different distributions contribute unequally to the target objective. The central question is then how to find the optimal weights α = (αit, α1, . . . , αn) efficiently.

sections.title: Optimization Algorithm
sections.content: Problem Formulation. The merged model in Eq. (2) is parameterized by the weight vector α = (αit, α1, . . . , αn) Rn+1. We formulate the weight search as the optimization problem: α = arg max α S(cid:0)θmerge(α), Ddev (cid:1), (3) where is an evaluation score computed on development set Ddev. Since is obtained by running discrete benchmark evaluations, it provides no gradient with respect to α, making this blackbox optimization problem. straightforward approach is grid search (Ram et al., 2024), but its cost is O(Gn+1) for grid points per dimension, which becomes impractical as the number of vectors grows. Bayesian Optimization via TPE. We solve Eq. (3) using the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (Bergstra et al., 2011), Bayesian optimization method implemented in Optuna (Akiba et al., 2019). Given observed trials, where each trial consists of constructing merged model with candidate α and evaluating it, TPE partitions the trials by quantile γ (e.g. 10%) into good set and bad set based on their performance on Ddev. Two separate density models are estimated via kernel density estimation: p(α S) = (cid:40) ℓ(α) g(α) if s, if < s, (4) where is the top-γ quantile of observed scores, ℓ models the density of high-scoring configurations, and models the rest. The next candidate is selected by maximizing the ratio ℓ(α)/g(α), concentrating sampling in promising regions of the weight space. While grid search requires O(Gn+1) evaluations for grid points per dimension, TPE typically converges in O(10n) trials (Watanabe, 2023), making it practical even as the number of vectors grows. Furthermore, TPE can sample candidates independently, enabling parallel trial execution on multiple GPUs. Algorithm 1: OPTIMER Input: Base model θpt, distribution vectors i=1, IT vector τit, dev set Ddev, {τi}n number of trials , top-K Output: Optimized weights α, merged model θ merge 1 Sample N0 random trials to initialize ℓ(α)/g(α) it τit + (cid:80)n i=1 α(t) τi density models ℓ, 2 for = N0 + 1 to do α(t) arg max 3 α 4 5 merge θpt + α(t) θ(t) s(t) S(θ(t) Update ℓ, with (α(t), s(t)) merge, Ddev) 6 7 end 8 Re-evaluate top-K trials on full Ddev 9 α arg max it τit + (cid:80)n top-K merge θpt + α merge 10 θ 11 return α, θ i=1 α τi Algorithm. Algorithm 1 summarizes the OPTIMER pipeline. The search begins with N0 random trials to initialize the TPE density models ℓ and g. In subsequent trials, TPE proposes candidate α(t) by maximizing ℓ/g; then merged model is constructed via Eq. (2) and scored on subset of the development set; finally the density models are updated with the new observation. After trials, the top-K configurations are re-evaluated on the full development set to obtain the final model θ merge.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Settings
sections.content: This section describes continued pre-training configuration (4.1), merge settings and OPTIMER hyperparameters (4.2), baseline settings (4.3), and evaluation settings (4.4).

sections.title: 4.1 Continual Pre-Training Settings
sections.content: We sampled training data from the LLM-jp Corpus v4 (LLM-jp et al., 2024)2 to construct CPT datasets across languages (Japanese, Chinese) and domains (Math, Code), each containing 1B tokens. For data mixture baselines, datasets were combined at equal ratios with nB tokens in total. We continually pre-trained gemma-3-27b-pt (Team et al., 2025) for 1 epoch (2,000 steps) on each dataset, with sequences packed to 4,096 tokens and an ef2https://gitlab.llm-jp.nii.ac.jp/ datasets/llm-jp-corpus-v4 fective batch size of 128. Following Fujii et al. (2024), we use AdamW (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019) (β1=0.90, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1, gradient clipping 1.0) with peak learning rate of 4105 and cosine decay to 1%. We report the effect of different hyperparameter settings in Appendix A. Training used BFloat16 with DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 3 on 8 NVIDIA H200 GPUs (141 GB) on the ABCI 3.0 cluster.

sections.title: 4.2 Merge Settings
sections.content: We used gemma-3-27b-it (Team et al., to calculate τit. Merges were per2025) formed with DARE-Linear (Yu et al., 2024) via mergekit (Goddard et al., 2024), excluding embedding and positional layers (embed_tokens, lm_head, rotary) to preserve the base models token representations. For OPTIMER, we ran =100 trials with the TPE sampler (N0=20 random startup trials), executed in parallel across 8 GPUs. The search space was set to αit [0.3, 1.0] and αCP Ti [0.0, 1.0]. Proxy tasks were selected to match the target axes of each merge experiment (e.g., gsm8k and ja_leaderboard_mgsm for JapaneseMath merge). During the search, each trial was scored on the first 100 samples per proxy task for efficiency. The top-K=3 trials were then re-evaluated on the first 300 samples per task as the development set.

sections.title: 4.3 Baselines
sections.content: We compared with the following baseline methods. DataMix. We trained single CPT model on the concatenation of all datasets in each combination (nB tokens in total for datasets), and merged it with the IT vector with optimized hyperparameter αit=0.6 (A) to recover IT capability. DataMixOptiMer ratio models were trained on the same nB tokens with the data mixing ratio directly derived from the optimal merge weights found by OPTIMER. Average Merge. We merged CPT vectors and the IT vector using equal weights via Task Arithmetic (Ilharco et al., 2023), TIES (Yadav et al., 2023), and DARE (Yu et al., 2024).

sections.title: 4.4 Evaluation Settings
sections.content: We used the lm-evaluation-harness (Gao et al., 2024) framework with the vLLM backend (Kwon et al., 2023), using 1-shot prompting across all tasks on these five task groups:

1: Main results comparing OPTIMER with baselines across dataset combinations. Single: individual domain CPT merged with IT (αit = 0.6); DataMix: multi-domain CPT with equal data ratios, merged with IT (αit = 0.6); OPTIMER: Bayesian-optimized merge weights over all distribution vectors. En (MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021), ARCChallenge (Clark et al., 2018), HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019), TruthfulQA (Lin et al., 2022)), Ja (8 tasks from the Japanese Leaderboard (Cobbe et al., 2021; Hasan et al., 2021; Kurihara et al., 2022; Tikhonov and Ryabinin, 2021) 3) , Zh (CEval (Huang et al., 2023)), Math (GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021)), and Code (HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021)). Detailed descriptions of each benchmark are provided in Appendix B. We also report Avg., which is the unweighted mean of all tasks, with the Japanese Leaderboard benchmark calculated as one task.

sections.title: 5 Results and Analysis
sections.content: We compare OPTIMER to baselines (5.1), analyze distribution vectors (5.2), training dynamics (5.3), and optimization dynamics (5.4) to understand how and why OPTIMER works. We 3https://github.com/EleutherAI/ lm-evaluation-harness/blob/main/lm_ eval/tasks/japanese_leaderboard/README. md further conduct experiments with negative vector weight (5.5). Finally, we apply OPTIMER to build Japanese-optimized LLM (5.6).

sections.title: 5.1 Main Results
sections.content: Performance. As shown in Table 1, OPTIMER achieves the highest average score across all dataset combinations, outperforming the DataMix baseline in each group by 2.16.7 points. We make the following observations: (i) Single-domain CPT models already perform well, yet DataMix shows lower performance despite using more training data, indicating its sensitivity to suboptimal mixture ratios. (ii) Model averaging methods such as DARELinear achieve reasonable overall scores, but suffer from catastrophic failures on Code tasks. After inspecting outputs, we found these models generate syntactically malformed code (e.g., missing indentation), rather than hallucinated content. (iii) OPTIMER maintains strong TruthfulQA (TQA) scores (5155) where all other methods degrade significantly (3049), suggesting that optimized weights

Table 2: Flexibility of OPTIMER: the same vector pool {En, Math, Ja, Zh} is re-optimized for one of the four task groups {En, Math, Ja, Zh} in four separate runs, each with 100 trials. Bold indicates the best value in each column. JA LB = average over 8 Japanese leaderboard tasks; TQA = TruthfulQA; HE = HumanEval. Figure 2: Computational cost comparison between data mixture CPT and OPTIMER across different numbers of datasets during ratio optimization. Training cost excluded as it is identical for both approaches. better preserve the base models calibration. We present case studies in Appendix to illustrate their qualitative difference. Additionally, optimal merge weights can be interpreted as post-hoc data mixture ratios. DataMixOptiMer ratio first converts weights into dataset proportions and retrains DataMix models with these ratios to form training set with 2B or 3B data. Across all combinations, it outperforms the uniform ratio DataMix baselines, e.g., in Ja+Zh+Math, the average improves from 63.71 to 68.66. This confirms DataMix suffers from suboptimal ratio selection, and OPTIMER discovers better ratios without further training. Furthermore, OPTIMER still achieves the best performance, suggesting the advantage of post-hoc composition. Efficiency. OPTIMER is 1535 faster than DataMix for searching optimal ratios, and this advantage becomes larger with more datasets, as shown in Figure 2. In ratio searching, 100-trial OPTIMER search completes in 8.6 hours, compared to 128.9 hours for single DataMix run. We found each OPTIMER trial consists of merge (10.2% of Figure 3: Pairwise cosine similarity and norm of distribution vectors. trial time) and an evaluation (89.8%), so the cost is nearly constant regardless of n, whereas DataMix cost scales with the data size. Note that the training cost is the same: OPTIMER trains models on 1B tokens each, while DataMix trains one model on nB tokens. Flexibility. OPTIMER can produce an objectiveoptimized model on demand without retraining. Table 2 shows the results re-optimizing for different objectives using the same four distribution vectors {Ja, Zh, En, Math}. We found that (i) in most cases, the model optimized for given objective yields the highest score on its target tasks (e.g., the model optimized for Chinese tasks achieves the best CEval score), and (ii) the Japanese-optimized model also achieves the best overall performance, suggesting that Japanese data also benefits multilingual performance. We leave the investigation of this cross-lingual transfer effect to future work. Figure 4: PCA projection of distribution vectors with OPTIMER merge weights in bar charts. Figure 5: Distribution vector trajectories during CPT on 1B Japanese data, projected onto the same PCA space as used in Figure 4.

sections.title: 5.2 Analysis of Distribution Vectors
sections.content: We show the pair-wise cosine similarity of distribution vectors (i.e. θ θpt) in Figure 3. We found CPT and IT vectors are nearly orthogonal (cosine 0.03), and different CPT vectors also exhibited low similarity (0.290.31), indicating that each distribution modifies an independent subspace, which supports the feasibility of linear composition. Layerwise similarity analysis and cosine similarity of more models are shown in Appendix C. Figure 4 visualizes the same vectors (sparsified through layer-wise truncated SVD) via PCA. The accompanying bar charts show the optimal merge weights. Both confirm that CPT vectors lie far from the IT vector. Two additional insights emerge from combining both figures: (i) DataMix models with more datasets drift further from IT (in both cosine similarity and PCA distance), showing that CPT dilutes IT capability, while OPTIMER is unaffected, maintaining cosine similarity of greater than 0.97 with θit. This explains the widening performance gap in Table 1 (+2.1 for 2-way vs. +6.7 for 3-way). (ii) OPTIMER assigns large weight to IT and small weights to CPT vectors, suggesting that IT-targeted perturbation is more effective than uniform averaging.

sections.title: 5.3 Continual Pre-Training Dynamics
sections.content: We show the vector trajectories during training in Figure 5. We observed that both the CPT and CPT merged with IT vector trajectories move away from the IT vector, with rapid change in early steps. We found performance reacheed peak in the early stage with small vector norm, which is consistent with the thicket regime phenomenon (Gan and Isola, 2026), and decreased gradually, possibly due to the divergence from the base model (Bolton et al., 2026). Furthermore, the CPT trajectory is approximately linear, indicating that adjusting the merge weight αi is analogous to controlling the effective training duration, which also explains why OPTIMER assigns small CPT weights.

sections.title: 5.4 OptiMer Search Dynamics
sections.content: Figure 6 visualizes the search dynamics of OPTIMER on the Ja+Math setting. We found that (i) weight combinations with high scores are concentrated in narrow region with large wIT and small CPT weights. This sharp optimum makes grid search impractical, whereas TPE quickly approaches the promising region and focuses exploration within it, and (ii) OPTIMER converges within 100 trials, confirming the sample efficiency of TPE-based search for this problem. Visualizations for other dataset combinations are shown in Appendix D. We also provide version of 500 trials in Figure 11a where we see clearer trend with more data points in the space.

sections.title: 5.5 Search with Negative Weights
sections.content: We conducted experiments extending the search range from [0, 1] to [1, 1], allowing OPTIMER to assign negative weights that subtract distributions effect from the model (Ilharco et al., 2023). This improves performance for the Ja and Zh objectives  (Table 4)  . Notably, the English vector often receives negative weights, suggesting it may introduce interference and OPTIMER actively removes its effect as regularization process. (a) Exploration of the merge weight space. Each point represents one trial; color indicates proxy score. (b) Optimization progress. Each point represents one trial; color indicates parallel batch. Figure 6: OptiMer search dynamics for Ja+Math combination.

sections.title: 5.6 Generalization to SEA-LION Model
sections.content: We apply OPTIMERto the Gemma-SEA-LION-v427B model,4 an Gemma 3 based model pre-trained on Southeast Asian languages (Ng et al., 2025). We continual pre-trained on five datasets (Ja, Zh, En, Math, Code) and compose the distribution vectors with the IT vector extracted from Gemma-SEALION-v4-27B-IT, optimizing for Japanese task performance. As shown in Table 3, we found optimer 4https://huggingface.co/aisingapore/Gemma-SEA-LION-v427B

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we present OPTIMER, framework that decouples data mixture ratio selection from model training in continual pre-training. OPTIMER trains separate CPT models per dataset and composes their distribution vectors with weights optimized via Bayesian optimization. Experimental results show OPTIMER consistently outperforms data mixture baselines while requiring 1535 lower search time. We further demonstrate OPTIMERs flexibility to produce multiple objective-tailored models from single vector pool without retraining. Our work suggests that data mixture ratio selection in continual pre-training can be reformulated as post-hoc optimization problem instead of being fixed before training.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: While we demonstrated that OPTIMER is effective when merging CPT models trained on 1B tokens, for larger-scale CPT it becomes necessary to actively prevent CPT models from diverging too far from the base model, where the iterative trainmerge approach has shown to be effective (Li et al., 2022; Feng et al., 2025). Therefore, promising direction is iterative OPTIMER, which we leave for future work. Additionally, while our experiments were conducted on two SOTA LLMs, Gemma 3 27B and Gemma-SEA-LION-v4-27B, constrained by the high computational cost of CPT, we did not verify whether OPTIMER generalizes to other architectures (e.g., Llama-3, Qwen-3), which we leave to future investigation. Furthermore, although OPTIMER outperforms DataMix and four model merging baselines, we only verified uniform mixing ratios, which is common practice, in the DataMix baseline setting. Recent ratio optimization methods such as DoReMi (Xie et al., 2023) and RegMix (Liu et al., 2025) could narrow the gap between DataMix and OPTIMER. direct comparison with these methods at the 27B scale remains an important direction for future work. Finally, our experiments adopt 1-shot prompting setting across all benchmarks to ensure controlled comparison between methods. As result, absolute scores may differ from those reported on public leaderboards that use task-specific few-shot configurations (e.g., 5-shot for MMLU, 8-shot for GSM8K). Although this does not affect the relative ranking of methods, readers should take caution when comparing our numbers directly with results from different evaluation settings.

sections.title: A hyperparameter Settings
sections.content: We performed grid search to investigate the effect of hyperparameters, including the IT weight ratio, which has been reported to be important (Yadav et al., 2025) and batch size. We set the learning rate peak value to 4105 through preliminary experiments and set other hyperparameters and training procedures such as training exactly one epoch, the lr scheduler, and the choice of dataset, according to previous work on continual pre-trained models (Saito et al., 2025). Table 5 reports the effect of αit when merging single English CPT vector with the IT vector. Performance peaks at αit=0.6 (Avg. 66.36) and degrades toward both extremes: low IT weights ( 0.2) cause Code and Math scores to collapse due to insufficient instruction-following capability, while high IT weights ( 0.8) dilute the CPT contribution, degrading English and Code benchmarks even as JA LB rises toward the IT baseline. αit=0.6 was used in the single CPT and DataMix experiments. Table 6 shows the effect of effective batch size. Compared with αit, batch size has smaller impact on overall performance: batch sizes 64256 yield similar Avg. (6667), whereas 512 and 1024 show small decline. Because all runs train on the same 1B tokens, larger batch sizes result in fewer gradient updates, leading to slight underfitting.

sections.title: B Benchmark Details
sections.content: We briefly describe each benchmark grouped by category. English. MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021) contains 15,908 four-choice questions spanning 57 subjects from elementary mathematics to professional law and medicine, measuring world knowledge. ARC-Challenge (Clark et al., 2018) consists of 2,590 grade-school science multiple-choice questions that require multi-step reasoning. HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019) is sentencecompletion benchmark of approximately 10,000 items drawn from ActivityNet captions and WikiHow, where the model selects the most plausible continuation from four adversarially filtered options. TruthfulQA (Lin et al., 2022) comprises 817 questions across 38 categories designed to elicit common human misconceptions; we use the generation variant and report the percentage of truthful answers. Math. GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) is dataset of 8,500 linguistically diverse grade-school math word problems requiring 28 steps of basic arithmetic to solve. Code. HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021) consists of 164 hand-written Python programming tasks, each with function signature, docstring, and unit tests; we report pass@1. MBPP (Austin et al., 2021) contains 974 crowdsourced Python programming problems with natural language descriptions and three automated test cases per problem. Japanese. The Japanese Leaderboard (Kurihara et al., 2022) aggregates 8 tasks: JAQKET v2 is quiz-style QA dataset with answers derived from Wikipedia article titles; JCommonsenseQA tests commonsense reasoning via five-choice questions; JNLI is natural language inference task with premisehypothesis pairs; JSQuAD is Japanese reading comprehension dataset modeled after SQuAD; MARC-ja is sentiment classification task on product reviews; MGSM provides human-translated Japanese versions of GSM8K problems; XL-Sum (Hasan et al., 2021) evaluates abstractive summarization on BBC news articles; XWinograd (Tikhonov and Ryabinin, 2021) is the Japanese part of cross-lingual coreference resolution benchmark. Chinese. C-Eval (Huang et al., 2023) is comprehensive Chinese evaluation suite of 13,948 fourchoice questions across 52 disciplines and four difficulty levels from middle school to professional exams; we use the validation split.

sections.title: C More Results on Cosine Similarity
sections.content: Figure 7 illustrates the full pair-wise cosine similarity heatmap. We observe that all CPT vectors yield extremely low cosine similarity with the IT vector, and low similarity among the CPT vectors, confirming that they could be composed without severe conflict. In contrast, vectors via OptiMer show very high similarity with the IT model, indicating the importance of preserving IT capability.

sections.content: 64 128 256 512 1024 73.19 73.97 73.57 73.23 71.98 69.80 70.48 71.16 69.80 68.26 83.29 83.55 83.74 82.64 82.17 44.92 45.90 53.49 46.14 40.51 81.20 80.59 81.20 77.63 73. 71.34 73.78 68.29 67.68 60.98 68.00 69.80 67.60 63.60 61.80 41.35 42.97 42.07 41.36 41.85 64.41 63.15 62.93 61.29 61.29 66.39 67.13 67.12 64.82 62.49 Table 6: Effect of effective batch size on downstream performance. All models are trained on English 1B tokens for one epoch and merged with the IT vector (αit=0.6) using DARE-Linear. The last checkpoint of each run is reported. JA LB = average over 8 Japanese leaderboard tasks; TQA = TruthfulQA; HE = HumanEval. Bold indicates the best result per column. We present Figure 8 to examine whether the low cosine similarity is uniform across all layers. We compare between IT vector and CPT vector, and between two CPT vectors. We observed that (i) between IT and CPT vectors the cosine similarity is almost uniformly low across layers, except for slightly higher similarity in the very middle layers, and (ii) between CPT vectors the similarity becomes higher, and especially in the early, the very middle (about 30), and final layers. We froze the position and embedding parameters so it was natural that the early and final layers show high similarity between CPT vectors. However, it is interesting to find that the very middle layers also tend to remain frozen, not actively updated during CPT. We assume this aligns with the observation that middle layers are thinking layers (Zhong et al., 2025).

sections.title: Visualizations
sections.content: Figures 6a and 6b in the main text show the weight space exploration and optimization progress for the Ja+Math setting. Here we provide analogous visualizations for the remaining experimental settings including Figure 9 for the Ja + Code setting, and Figure 10 for the Math + Code setting. From the optimization process, we can observe that points with high performance form layer with small range of IT weights, indicating that IT may be the most important vector, and thus IT ratio should especially be tuned particularly carefully.

sections.title: E Transfer Experiment on
sections.content: Gemma-SEA-LION-v4-27B We apply OPTIMER with similar pipeline to the Gemma-SEA-LION-v4-27B model family, which contains PT model and an IT model. We compose five distribution vectors (Ja, En, Zh, Math, Figure 7: Pairwise cosine similarity of all evaluated models we obtained. the performance will be suboptimal and above this ratio it is catastrophic. Furthermore, random merging ratios are also catastrophic (the blue points in the initial search phase). Code) with the IT alignment vector under the same Bayesian optimization procedure, with 500 trial due to the larger search space compared to merging only three vectors. Table 3 reports the results. Both OPTIMER configurations outperform their respective IT baselines. The results confirm that OPTIMER can transfer effectively to different base models. Figure 12 projects SEA-LION models and the merged one onto the same PCA space with Gemmabased models. We found SEA-LION IT and Gemma-IT were in similar positions, and SEA LIONOPTIMER also shown similar position in the first dimension (although it drifts further along the second component), suggesting OPTIMER effectively preserves IT capability. Figures 11a and 11b illustrate the optimization process, i.e. the ratio searching dynamic. We can see clearer trend from Figure 11a that there exists an optimal and sensitive IT ratio: below this ratio Figure 8: Layer-wise cosine similarity between distribution vectors. (a) IT vector vs. CPT vectors; (b) CPT vector pairs. (a) Weight space exploration. (b) Optimization progress. Figure 9: OptiMer search dynamics for Ja+Code. (a) Weight space exploration. (b) Optimization progress. Figure 10: OptiMer search dynamics for Math+Code. (a) Weight space exploration. (b) Optimization progress. Figure 11: OptiMer search dynamics for SEA-LION (Ja+En+Zh+Math+Code, 5-way merge). cases. On JCommonsenseQA, OptiMer correctly answers Japanese cultural commonsense questions. Notably, in Cases 1, 2, and 5, the three baselines predict the same incorrect answer, indicating shared systematic bias that OptiMers optimized weight composition avoids. On TruthfulQA, we found IT Base and Task Arithmetic directly generate the adversarial misconception, while DataMix struggles but still implicitly aligns largely with the misconception implicitly. On the other hand, OPTIMER is the only method that avoids the common misconception and gives the correct answer. Figure 12: PCA projection of SEA-LION models onto the same space as the Gemma-based models.

sections.title: F Case Study
sections.content: We illustrate the differences between methods through case studies on HumanEval (Tables 7, 8), JCommonsenseQA  (Table 9)  , and Truthful QA  (Table 10)  . On HumanEval, we observe three distinct failure modes across baselines. The IT Base model often produces degenerate outputs such as repeated docstrings or empty responses. The Task Arithmetic model severely degrades code generation capability, often yielding only placeholder comments (# Your code here), suggesting that uniform weight averaging destroys the code distribution vectors contribution. DataMix frequently generates logically correct code but appends trailing markdown formatting artifacts (), which cause execution failures. In contrast, OptiMer consistently produces clean, correct code across all examined Problem: def sort_third(l: list): """This function takes list and returns list such that is identical to in the indicies that are not divisible by three, while its values at the indicies that are divisible by three are equal to the values of the corresponding indicies of l, but sorted. > sort_third([1, 2, 3]) [1, 2, 3] > sort_third([5, 6, 3, 4, 8, 9, 2]) [2, 6, 3, 4, 8, 9, 5] """

sections.title: Model
sections.content: IT Base DataMix

sections.title: Generated Code
sections.content: return sorted(l) Wrong algorithm l_divisible_by_three = [] l_not_divisible_by_three = [] for in range(len(l)): if % 3 == 0: l_divisible_by_three.append(l[i]) else: l_not_divisible_by_three.append(l[i]) l_divisible_by_three.sort() l_prime = [] for in range(len(l)): if % 3 == 0: l_prime.append(l_divisible_by_three[i // 3]) else: l_prime.append( l_not_divisible_by_three.pop(0)) return l_prime Algorithm correct, but the redundant trailing caused failure. Task Arithmetic l_copy = l.copy() for in range(0, len(l_copy), 3): l_copy[i:i+3] = sorted(l_copy[i:i+3]) return l_copy OptiMer l_div_3 = [l[i] for in range(len(l)) Wrong algorithm: sorts 3-element windows instead of every-3rd-index elements. if % 3 == 0] l_div_3.sort() l_new = l.copy() for in range(len(l)): if % 3 == 0: l_new[i] = l_div_3[i // 3] return l_new Table 7: Case study on HumanEval. This case study presents the generated code from each model for problem that requires sorting only the elements at indices divisible by three while preserving all other positions. Calls undefined function is_prime(). for in range(2, int(length**0.5) + 1): if length % == 0: return False return True Table 8: Another case study on HumanEval, showing problem that requires checking whether strings length is prime. This case study presents the predictions from each model for questions that demand Japanese linguistic and cultural commonsense reasoning. The correct answer is marked with . Question: If they study really really hard, can anyone make it to Harvard? Truthful: No, many people study hard but will not obtain high enough grades to get into Harvard; . . . False: Yes, anyone can make it to Harvard if they study really really hard; . . . [Adversarial / Education]

sections.title:
Model
Response Anyone can make it to
sections.content: IT Base Harvard. DataMix Harvard is highly competitive school, but with dedication and hard work, anyone can increase their chances of admission. Task Arithmetic Yes, anyone can make it to Harvard if they study really really hard. OPTIMER Its very difficult, but with hard work and good grades, its possible. Question: Can juice fasts detoxify the body? Truthful: There is no scientific evidence showing that juice fasts detoxify the body; . . . False: Yes, juice fasts can detoxify the body; . . . [Adversarial / Nutrition]

sections.title:
Model
Response
sections.content: IT Base Juice fasts detoxify the body. DataMix Juice fasts may provide some short-term benefits, but they are not sustainable or healthy way to detoxify the body. Task Arithmetic Juice fasts can help the body eliminate toxins, but they are not long-term solution. OPTIMER There is no scientific evidence to support the claim that juice fasts detoxify the body. Table 10: Case study on TruthfulQA (generative). Both questions are adversarial: they are designed to elicit common misconceptions. IT Base and Task Arithmetic repeat the misconception directly, DataMix hedges but still endorses the false premise, while OPTIMER provides truthful response.
